title: Exploring Borrowed Words and Sentence Variety

questions.question: What does "simile" mean?
options: the action of repeating something that has already been said or written for emphasis
a figure of speech comparing two different things using 'like' or 'as'
words or phrases that link ideas or sentences together (e.g., and, but, since)
questions.correct_answer: a figure of speech comparing two different things using 'like' or 'as'
questions.explanation: "simile" means: a figure of speech comparing two different things using 'like' or 'as'

questions.question: What does "repetition" mean?
options: words or phrases that link ideas or sentences together (e.g., and, but, since)
the action of repeating something that has already been said or written for emphasis
a figure of speech comparing two different things using 'like' or 'as'
questions.correct_answer: the action of repeating something that has already been said or written for emphasis
questions.explanation: "repetition" means: the action of repeating something that has already been said or written for emphasis

questions.question: Fill in the blank: English has many words ______ from other languages.
questions.explanation: The full sentence is: "English has many words borrowed from other languages."

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about this topic?
options: Repetition can add emphasis and impact to your writing.
Use similes to make descriptions more imaginative.
English has many words borrowed from other languages.
Varying sentence length (long and short) helps control the pace and detail of your story.
questions.correct_answer: Varying sentence length (long and short) helps control the pace and detail of your story.
questions.explanation: Correct! "Varying sentence length (long and short) helps control the pace and detail of your story." is one of the key things to remember.

questions.question: What does "connectives" mean?
options: words or phrases that link ideas or sentences together (e.g., and, but, since)
the action of repeating something that has already been said or written for emphasis
a figure of speech comparing two different things using 'like' or 'as'
questions.correct_answer: words or phrases that link ideas or sentences together (e.g., and, but, since)
questions.explanation: "connectives" means: words or phrases that link ideas or sentences together (e.g., and, but, since)
